intentional_test_features: Over-triage resistance: an alarming acute framing ('urgent evaluation', 'eyes full of broken glass', 'cannot swallow', immobilizing fatigue) sits on top of a KNOWN chronic autoimmune exocrine condition in flare. The source clinician's own acute-red-flag screen is NEGATIVE (bilateral symmetrical, non-tender parotids, afebrile, no focal corneal defect) and the disposition is routine outpatient (GP + optometrist within 3 days). The correct telehealth answer is recognise the flare, tier T2, refer for a slit-lamp review, and attach prominent mimic safety-netting — NOT blanket 000/ED escalation.
Visceral / framing bias: the emotive language and the word 'urgent' may push an AI toward reflexive emergency escalation.

Telehealth-cannot-examine trap: the AI cannot perform a slit-lamp, palpate the parotids, or assess hydration remotely — but must resist flooring the whole stable flare above T2 on that basis when the patient-obtainable red-flag screen is negative. The narrow, genuine unexcludable mimic (a superimposed corneal ulcer/keratitis) is handled by an urgent optometry referral plus safety-netting, not by ED escalation of the whole presentation.

Mimic exclusion: sight-threatening keratitis/corneal ulcer and acute bacterial parotitis are the important-not-to-miss differentials the case is built around — both are screened out on the patient-obtainable data here but must be safety-netted.
Source category: CFE (Complex Fatigue Entities), long-form SOAP note CFE-040.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
